prompt:
Research query: How does damp heat aging affect the adhesion strength of DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics?

Below are evidence statements from a literature review. Extract ALL named studies, trials, and author references mentioned.

EVIDENCE:
- EVA laminates failed in interfacial manner independent of damp heat aging time throughout 10,000h exposure
- POE laminates exhibited failure mode transition from interfacial (unaged) to combined interfacial and cohesive after 10,000h damp heat
- POE laminates revealed improved compressive shear performance after damp heat aging compared to unaged state
- Higher lamination temperature was beneficial for peel force and gel content before and after aging for cross-linking encapsulants like EVA and POE
- TPO encapsulant had consistent peel force of about 160 N/cm independent of peak lamination temperature
- EVA laminates showed less degradation in peel force after damp heat than POE samples across tested lamination temperatures
- Kinetic model describes adhesion degradation rate as proportional to moisture concentration at the interface with Arrhenius temperature dependence
- Temperature and RH both accelerate adhesion degradation, with temperature acceleration rate faster than humidity
- Activation energy for adhesion degradation between backsheet and encapsulant is approximately 51 kJ/mol via Arrhenius analysis
- Interface strength reduction, microstructure defects, and polymer matrix hydrolysis are irreversible effects of damp-heat aging
- Damp-heat aging of BFRP joints tested at 80C/95%RH for 0, 10, 20, and 30 days using epoxy adhesives Araldite 2015 and 2012
- Tensile strength of adhesive joints decreased about 31.3% after moisture absorption and 54.4% under combined high temperature 95C and humid environment
- PEVA films with 18, 24, 33, and 40% VA content were cured at 150 C under vacuum and subjected to damp heat at 85C/85%RH per IEC 61215 for 1000 and 2000 hours
- Tensile strength, thermal stability and crosslinking were greater for PEVA with lower VA content (18 and 24%) and diminished for higher VA (33 and 40%) after DH aging
- Results recommend reducing VA content in PEVA to 24% for better encapsulation response against damp heat aging
- Thermal aging via 5,000 thermocycles induced a significant decrease in micro-tensile bond strength values with all seven adhesive systems tested
- One-step self-etch adhesives obtained the lowest MTBS results after both aging treatments with significantly reduced adhesive capacity after thermocycling
- The resistance of resin-dentin bonds to thermal-aging degradation was material dependent
- Three-way ANOVA showed substrate (p=0.00) and aging method (p=0.00) significantly affected bond strength but adhesive approach did not (p=0.84)
- Adhesive/mixed failures were predominant for all groups regardless of aging method
- Butyl rubber edge seal has two orders of magnitude lower WVTR than UV-curable epoxies and polymeric encapsulants
- Stiff Surlyn with 394 MPa elastic modulus caused PSC delamination by pulling up on ITO layer creating moisture ingress pathways
- EVA encapsulant decomposes into acetic acid under damp heat which reacts with perovskite causing yellow PbI2 degradation
- Adhesion energy Gc decreased after 5000 h of hot-dry (90 degrees C, ~1% RH) and hot-humid (90 degrees C, 60% RH) aging on cell/encapsulant/glass coupons.
- POE coupons demonstrated the best adhesion stability, followed by EPE composites and then EVA.
- FTIR-ATR showed insignificant bulk degradation, confirming the critical role of UV and moisture in accelerating encapsulant degradation.
- Small-scale laminates with EVA, POE, and TPO encapsulants were tested at peak lamination temperatures of 140, 160, and 180 degrees C.
- POE and EVA laminates showed higher peel forces at higher lamination temperatures, while TPO showed consistent peel forces independent of temperature.
- Laminates were exposed to DH for 1500h and 3000h to evaluate multilayer composite stability.
- PEVA18 showed inadequate transmittance and high stiffness after 2000 h of DH aging, discouraging its use as encapsulant.
- Results encourage the PV industry to reduce VA content in PEVA to 24% for better encapsulation response against DH aging.
- PEVA films with 18, 24, 33, and 40% VA content were cured at 150 degrees C under vacuum and subjected to damp-heat aging at 85 degrees C and 85% RH per IEC 61215 for 1000 and 2000 h.
- Encapsulant crosslinking increased and crystallinity decreased under hot-aerobic aging at 90 degrees C and 22% RH without UV or crosslinking initiators.
- Thermal degradation of the interface can be significant even when bulk encapsulant shows negligible degradation without UV and elevated humidity.
- EVA at the glass interface degrades more than at the cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes preferential for delamination.
- Both field and accelerated aging reveal a distinct adhesion plateau after 1 year in field and after 1,000 hours in a chamber.
- At 10,000 hours accelerated aging, EVA adhesion falls to a level generally seen after long-term field exposures of more than 15 years.
- Dentin bond strengths of dental adhesive systems decreased with increasing relative humidity across six conditions.
- Tracy et al. proposed threshold debonding energy of 160 J/m2 for encapsulant interfaces and 10 J/m2 for backsheet interfaces to avoid delamination.
- Optimal gel content range for tested EVA materials was 84-90%, with samples below 70% showing low chemical/optical stability and weak adhesion.
- STR (EVA manufacturer) suggests minimum 53 N/cm peel strength after lamination by 180-degree peel to ensure 20-year lifetime.
- Only 69% of BOMs tested degraded by less than 6% power after damp heat testing in the 2024 PVEL Scorecard.
- SEM observation showed smooth glass fiber surfaces in GF/PEI-145 specimens, indicating obvious separation between glass fiber and PEI matrix at high aging temperatures, confirming interfacial adhesion degradation as the primary failure mechanism.

For each named study, output ONE line in this format:
STUDY: <first_author_last_name> | <year_if_known> | <short_description>

Examples:
STUDY: Trepanowski | 2017 | 12-month ADF vs daily calorie restriction RCT
STUDY: Varady | 2022 | meta-analysis of alternate-day fasting protocols
STUDY: Longo | 2016 | fasting-mimicking diet clinical trial

Output ONLY the STUDY lines. If no named studies are found, output: NONE
